subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: You need one chessboard with an 8 by 8 grid of alternating light and dark squares. There are 32 pieces in total, 16 white and 16 black. Each side has 1 King, 1 Queen, 2 Rooks, 2 Bishops, 2 Knights, and 8 Pawns. The King is the tallest piece and usually has a cross on top. The Queen is the second tallest and wears a crown. Rooks look like castle towers, Bishops have a pointed slit near the top, and Knights are shaped like horse heads. Pawns are the smallest and most numerous. Optionally, you can use a chess clock for timed play.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Place the board between the two players so that each player has a light-colored square in their near-right corner. This is the most common setup mistake, so double-check it before placing any pieces. The columns running away from you are called files and are labeled a through h from White's left to right. The rows running side to side are called ranks and are numbered 1 through 8 starting from White's side. Every square on the board has a unique name made from its file letter and rank number, like e4 or d7. White's pieces will go on ranks 1 and 2, while Black's pieces will go on ranks 7 and 8.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player arranges their 16 pieces on their two closest rows. Start with the back rank, which is row 1 for White and row 8 for Black. From left to right, place the pieces in this order: Rook, Knight, Bishop, Queen, King, Bishop, Knight, Rook. Here is the single most important setup rule: the Queen always goes on her own color. The white Queen starts on a light square, and the black Queen starts on a dark square. If your Queens are not on matching colors, something is wrong. Once the back rank is set, place all 8 Pawns in a straight line on the row directly in front, which is row 2 for White and row 7 for Black.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: White always takes the first move. To decide who plays White, you can flip a coin, agree between yourselves, or follow a tournament assignment. In casual games, players usually alternate colors after each game so that both sides get an equal number of turns as White.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: Players take turns one at a time, starting with White. On your turn you must make exactly one legal move. You pick up one of your own pieces and move it to a new square according to that piece's movement rules. If your piece lands on a square that is occupied by one of your opponent's pieces, that opponent's piece is captured and removed from the board permanently. You can never land on a square occupied by one of your own pieces. You may never skip your turn or pass. If you have any legal move available, you must make one.

subtopics.title: Piece Movement
subtopics.content:
The King moves exactly one square in any direction, whether horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. However, the King may never move onto a square that is attacked by an opponent's piece.

The Queen is the most powerful piece on the board. She moves any number of squares in a straight line, horizontally, vertically, or diagonally, but she cannot jump over other pieces in her path.

The Rook moves any number of squares in a straight line, either horizontally or vertically. Like the Queen, it cannot jump over pieces.

The Bishop moves any number of squares diagonally. It cannot jump over other pieces. Because it always stays on diagonals, each Bishop is permanently locked to the color of square it starts on. One of your Bishops will always travel on light squares and the other on dark squares for the entire game.

The Knight moves in an L-shape: two squares in one direction and then one square perpendicular, or one square in one direction and then two squares perpendicular. The Knight is the only piece that can jump over other pieces, which makes it especially useful in crowded positions.

The Pawn moves one square straight forward and can never move backward or sideways. On its very first move from its starting position, a Pawn may optionally advance two squares forward instead of one. Pawns capture differently from how they move: they capture one square diagonally forward. When a Pawn reaches the far end of the board, the 8th rank for White or the 1st rank for Black, it must immediately be promoted to a Queen, Rook, Bishop, or Knight of the same color. Most players choose a Queen.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
When a piece directly attacks the opponent's King, the King is in check. The player whose King is in check must immediately deal with it in one of three ways: move the King to a safe square that is not attacked, block the attacking piece by placing one of your own pieces between the attacker and the King, or capture the attacking piece. No other move is allowed while your King is in check.

Castling is a special move that involves the King and one of the Rooks. The King slides two squares toward a Rook, and that Rook jumps over the King to land on the square the King crossed. You can castle toward the Kingside or the Queenside. All of the following must be true to castle: neither the King nor the chosen Rook has moved previously in the game, there are no pieces standing between them, the King is not currently in check, and the King does not pass through or land on any square that is attacked by an opponent's piece.

En passant is a special Pawn capture. If your opponent moves a Pawn two squares forward from its starting position and it lands directly beside one of your Pawns, you may capture it as though it had only moved one square forward. You must make this capture on your very next turn or the opportunity is lost forever.

The game is drawn in several situations. Stalemate occurs when the player whose turn it is has no legal move but is not in check. Threefold repetition occurs when the same board position appears three times with the same player to move. The fifty-move rule applies when both players have made 50 consecutive moves each without any capture or Pawn advance. A draw also results from insufficient material, meaning neither side has enough pieces to force checkmate, such as King against King or King and Bishop against King. Finally, both players may agree to a draw at any time.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content: The game ends immediately when a King is checkmated. Checkmate means the King is in check and there is no legal move that can escape it. There is no safe square to move to, no piece that can block the attack, and no way to capture the attacker. The player who delivers checkmate wins the game. A player may also resign at any time to concede the game without playing to checkmate. Remember that stalemate is a draw, not a win for either side. In tournament play, a win earns 1 point, a draw earns half a point for each player, and a loss earns 0 points.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: Your very first goal is to control the center of the board. Move the Pawn in front of your King or the Pawn in front of your Queen forward two squares to stake your claim on the center. Over the next few moves, develop your Knights and Bishops by moving them off the back rank to active squares where they influence the middle of the board. Castle as soon as the pieces between your King and Rook are out of the way so your King is safely tucked behind a wall of Pawns. Resist the temptation to bring your Queen out early because your opponent will chase her around with developing moves, gaining time at your expense. Once both Knights and both Bishops are developed and you have castled, try to connect your Rooks by clearing the back rank so they can support each other.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
One of the most common beginner errors is moving the same piece two or three times in the opening instead of developing other pieces. Every turn you spend repositioning one piece is a turn your opponent uses to bring out something new.

Another mistake is ignoring piece values when trading. Pieces have approximate point values that help you judge trades: the Queen is worth about 9 points, a Rook about 5, a Bishop or Knight about 3 each, and a Pawn is worth 1. Trading your Rook for your opponent's Knight loses you 2 points of material, which adds up quickly.

Leaving your King in the center without castling is also dangerous. An uncastled King is exposed to attacks down the open center files, so castle early to get it to safety.

Before deciding your own move, always look at what your opponent just did and ask yourself what it threatens. Many beginners lose pieces simply because they did not notice an incoming attack.

Finally, avoid pushing too many Pawns without purpose. Pawns cannot move backward, so every advance creates permanent weaknesses in the squares behind them. Move Pawns only when you have a clear reason.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
A fork is when one of your pieces attacks two or more enemy pieces at the same time. The opponent can only save one, so you win the other. Knights are especially good at forks because their unusual L-shaped movement lets them attack pieces that cannot attack them back. A classic example is a Knight forking the opponent's King and Rook, which forces the King to move and lets you capture the Rook for free.

A pin occurs when your piece threatens an opponent's piece that cannot move without exposing a more valuable piece behind it. For instance, if your Bishop aims at an opponent's Knight that is standing in front of their King, that Knight is pinned and effectively frozen in place because moving it would put the King in check.

Controlling the center squares, specifically d4, d5, e4, and e5, is the most important positional goal in chess. Pieces placed in or near the center reach more squares and can shift to either side of the board quickly. Throughout the game, look for opportunities to occupy or put pressure on these four key squares.

metadata.notes: Content rewritten to production quality. Source: FIDE Laws of Chess (2023). Voice-friendly prose format.
